definition: Entry-level EMS provider who provides immediate care
rationale: EMR is the entry-level EMS certification. EMRs provide immediate life-saving care until higher-level providers arrive. They are trained in basic assessment airway management CPR bleeding control and assisting with certain medications.

definition: EMS provider trained in basic life support
rationale: EMTs provide basic life support including advanced airway management additional medications and more comprehensive patient assessment. EMT is the certification level above EMR.

definition: Provider with training between EMT and Paramedic
rationale: AEMTs have training beyond EMT including IV access and additional medications. They bridge the gap between basic EMT and Paramedic level care.

definition: Highest level prehospital care provider
rationale: Paramedics are the highest level of prehospital care providing advanced life support including cardiac monitoring advanced airways IV medications and other interventions.

definition: Physician oversight of EMS care
rationale: Medical direction provides physician oversight of EMS systems. Online direct medical direction involves real-time physician consultation. Offline indirect direction includes protocols and standing orders.

definition: Written guidelines for patient care
rationale: Protocols are evidence-based written guidelines that direct EMS providers in patient care decisions. They are developed under medical direction and define scope of practice for specific situations.

definition: Pre-authorized treatments that can be performed without calling medical direction
rationale: Standing orders are protocols that authorize specific treatments without requiring real-time physician contact. They allow immediate intervention for time-critical conditions like cardiac arrest.

definition: Legal limits of what a provider can do based on certification
rationale: Scope of practice legally defines what procedures and treatments each certification level can perform. It is determined by state law and medical direction. Exceeding scope is illegal.

definition: Systematic process to improve patient care quality
rationale: Quality improvement involves analyzing care delivery and outcomes to identify areas for improvement. It includes case review protocol evaluation and training needs assessment.

definition: Central communication center that receives calls and sends units
rationale: Dispatch or communications center receives 911 calls determines appropriate response and sends EMS units. Dispatchers may provide pre-arrival instructions to callers.

definition: Guidance given by dispatchers to callers before EMS arrives
rationale: Pre-arrival instructions are directions given by trained dispatchers to 911 callers. They may include CPR instructions choking management and bleeding control while waiting for EMS.

definition: Verbal patient information transmitted to hospital
rationale: Radio report or hospital notification provides the receiving facility with patient information including condition vital signs treatments and ETA allowing them to prepare for arrival.

definition: Documentation of assessment and care provided
rationale: The PCR is the legal document recording all patient information assessment findings treatments and outcomes. It becomes part of the permanent medical record.

definition: Federal law protecting patient health information privacy
rationale: HIPAA Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act protects patient health information privacy. EMS providers must maintain confidentiality and only share information appropriately.

definition: Standardized words for letters to ensure clear communication
rationale: The phonetic alphabet Alpha Bravo Charlie etc ensures clear communication especially over radio. Each letter has a standard word to prevent confusion between similar-sounding letters.

definition: Equipment worn to protect against hazards
rationale: PPE includes gloves masks gowns and eye protection worn to protect providers from exposure to blood body fluids and infectious agents. Selection depends on the situation.

definition: Precautions to prevent contact with body fluids
rationale: BSI involves using appropriate barriers to prevent contact with blood and body fluids. It assumes all patients are potentially infectious. Gloves are minimum protection for all patient contact.

definition: Infection control practices used for all patients
rationale: Standard precautions are infection control practices applied to all patients regardless of suspected infection status. They include hand hygiene PPE safe injection practices and respiratory hygiene.

definition: Protection for diseases spread through the air
rationale: Airborne precautions protect against diseases spread by small particles that remain suspended in air like tuberculosis. They require N95 respirators and negative pressure rooms.

definition: Protection for diseases spread by large respiratory droplets
rationale: Droplet precautions protect against diseases spread by larger respiratory droplets that fall within 3-6 feet like influenza. Surgical masks provide adequate protection.

definition: Protection for diseases spread by direct contact
rationale: Contact precautions protect against diseases spread by direct or indirect contact like MRSA and C. difficile. They require gloves gowns and dedicated equipment.

definition: Dangerous substances that pose health or safety risks
rationale: Hazardous materials are substances that can cause harm to people or the environment. EMS providers should not enter hazmat scenes without proper training and equipment.

definition: Process of removing harmful substances from patients or equipment
rationale: Decontamination removes hazardous materials from patients clothing and equipment. Patients may need decontamination before treatment to protect providers and prevent facility contamination.

definition: Assessment and assurance that a scene is safe before entering
rationale: Scene safety is the first priority on every call. Providers assess for hazards like traffic violence hazmat and structural dangers before approaching patients. An injured provider cannot help.

definition: Forces and events that caused traumatic injury
rationale: MOI describes how traumatic injuries occurred including type and amount of force direction of impact and factors like speed height and restraint use. It helps predict injury patterns.

definition: Type and origin of medical condition
rationale: NOI describes what is wrong with a medical non-traumatic patient such as chest pain difficulty breathing or altered mental status. It guides assessment and treatment approach.

definition: Sorting patients by severity when resources are limited
rationale: Triage is the process of rapidly assessing and categorizing multiple patients to determine treatment and transport priority. It is used in mass casualty incidents when patients exceed resources.

definition: Simple Triage And Rapid Treatment system for MCIs
rationale: START is a triage system using RPM Respiration Perfusion Mental status to rapidly categorize patients as immediate delayed minor or deceased. It takes about 30 seconds per patient.

definition: Event with patients exceeding normal resources
rationale: An MCI is any incident where the number of patients exceeds immediately available resources. The definition is relative to available resources. MCIs require triage and resource management.

definition: Standardized emergency management structure
rationale: ICS is a standardized organizational structure for managing emergencies. It provides clear chain of command defined roles and scalability. It is used for all incidents from small to catastrophic.

definition: Person in charge of overall incident management
rationale: The Incident Commander has overall authority for managing an incident. Initially this may be the first-arriving unit but transfers to more qualified personnel as they arrive.

definition: Location where resources wait for assignment
rationale: The staging area is where EMS units personnel and equipment wait until assigned to specific tasks. It keeps resources organized and ready for deployment without clogging the scene.

definition: Process of freeing a patient from entrapment
rationale: Extrication is the process of safely removing patients trapped in vehicles or structures. It may involve specialized equipment and personnel. Patient care continues during extrication.

definition: Assistance provided by neighboring agencies
rationale: Mutual aid agreements allow agencies to request assistance from neighboring departments when local resources are overwhelmed. This provides surge capacity during major incidents.

definition: Failure to provide standard care causing harm
rationale: Negligence is failure to provide care that a reasonable provider would provide resulting in patient harm. It requires proving duty breach causation and damages.

definition: Terminating care without proper transfer
rationale: Abandonment is ending patient care without transferring to someone of equal or higher training or without patient consent. Once care begins it must continue until properly transferred.

definition: Creating fear of offensive contact
rationale: Assault in medical context means creating reasonable apprehension of harmful or offensive contact. Threatening to give treatment without consent could constitute assault.

definition: Unlawful physical contact without consent
rationale: Battery is actual harmful or offensive physical contact without consent. Treating a competent patient who refuses care could constitute battery.

definition: Direct verbal or written permission for treatment
rationale: Expressed consent is when a patient directly states or indicates permission for treatment. It may be verbal or written. Competent adults must give consent before treatment.

definition: Assumed consent when patient cannot give permission
rationale: Implied consent assumes an unconscious or incapacitated patient would consent to emergency care if able. It allows treatment of patients who cannot give expressed consent.

definition: Consent given after understanding treatment and risks
rationale: Informed consent requires that the patient understands the proposed treatment alternatives and risks before agreeing. Simply signing a form without understanding is not informed consent.

definition: Patient's right to decline treatment
rationale: Competent adults have the right to refuse treatment even if the decision seems unwise. Refusal must be informed and the patient must understand the consequences. Documentation is essential.

definition: Order directing that CPR not be performed
rationale: A DNR order indicates the patient does not wish to receive CPR if they stop breathing or their heart stops. It does not mean withhold all care. Other treatments may still be appropriate.

definition: Document expressing patient wishes for future care
rationale: Advance directives include living wills and healthcare powers of attorney. They document patient wishes for medical care if they become unable to make decisions.

definition: Legal protection for people who help in emergencies
rationale: Good Samaritan laws provide legal protection to people who voluntarily help others in emergencies. Protection typically requires acting in good faith within your training and without gross negligence.

definition: Legal obligation to provide care
rationale: Duty to act is the legal obligation to respond and provide care. It typically exists when EMS is dispatched and on duty. Off-duty personnel may not have the same duty depending on jurisdiction.

definition: Stress reactions after traumatic events
rationale: Critical incident stress involves emotional cognitive physical and behavioral reactions to traumatic events. EMS providers may experience these after particularly difficult calls.

definition: Structured group discussion after traumatic incidents
rationale: CISD is a structured group discussion led by trained facilitators after critical incidents. It helps normalize reactions and identify those who may need additional support.

definition: Physical and emotional exhaustion from chronic stress
rationale: Burnout is cumulative exhaustion from ongoing occupational stress. Symptoms include emotional exhaustion depersonalization and reduced sense of accomplishment. It develops over time.

definition: Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder from exposure to trauma
rationale: PTSD is a mental health condition triggered by experiencing or witnessing traumatic events. Symptoms include flashbacks nightmares avoidance and hyperarousal. Treatment is available and effective.
